title: Chocolate stout cake with cream-cheese top
subtitle: Single-tin loaf cake dark with stout and crowned with white frosting
excerpt: A dense, moist single-tin chocolate cake made with stout in the batter,the bitterness rounds the chocolate and the crumb takes on a malt depth that plain cocoa cakes never reach. Crowned with a thick layer of cream-cheese frosting that suggests the head on a pint glass. One bowl, one tin, about an hour from start to oven.
sourceNotes: The pattern of stout-and-chocolate cakes circulated in British and Irish home baking through the late twentieth century; the stout-in-the-batter trick is older, appearing in Victorian fruit-cake recipes that used dark ale for moisture. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932) has porter cakes in the Irish chapter. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136,the general single-tin cake template (creaming the butter and sugar, alternating dry and wet additions). The cream-cheese top is a twentieth-century American carrot-cake influence applied to the dark cake.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted cake whole, wrapped tightly, for up to 2 months. Defrost wrapped at room temperature and frost on the day of serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the cake the day before, cool fully, wrap, and frost on the morning of serving. The crumb improves overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 150°C. A moderate temperature lets the deep batter cook through without splitting the top.

recipeTools.notes: For melting the butter into the stout.
23 cm. A round springform makes unmoulding clean.
For spreading the frosting; a butter knife works.

glossaryTerms.definition: Soured cream brings acidity and fat to a cake batter at the same time. The acid weakens the gluten network and tenderises the crumb; the fat keeps the cake moist for several days. In dark chocolate cakes it also softens the bitterness of the cocoa. The cream itself should be at room temperature when added,cold cream will seize the warm butter mixture and the batter will look split.

content.text: A chocolate cake made with stout in the batter takes on a dark, malt-deepened flavour that no amount of extra cocoa can match. The stout sharpens the chocolate; the cocoa carries the colour; the
. One bowl, one springform tin, 20 minutes of mixing and 50 in the oven. Crowned with a thick layer of cream-cheese frosting,set thick and white, suggesting the head on a pint glass.

Use any dark stout. The brand printed on the bottle is less important than the colour in the glass,look for something near-opaque with a tan head. A milder porter will work but the cake will sit lighter on the tongue.
Heat the oven to 170°C conventional (150°C fan). Butter a 23 cm springform tin and line the base with baking paper. The cake is deep, so the lining matters,paper the base, do not skip.
Melt the stout, butter, and cocoa

Pour the stout into a medium saucepan and add the butter. Set over a low heat and warm gently until the butter has melted into the stout,about 5 minutes. Whisk the cocoa powder and caster sugar into the warm liquid until smooth and glossy. Take the pan off the heat and leave to cool for 10 minutes; the next step adds eggs and the mixture cannot be hot when they go in or they will scramble.

Whisk the soured cream, eggs, and vanilla together in a small bowl. Pour into the cooled stout mixture and whisk smooth. Sift the flour and bicarbonate of soda into the pan and whisk until just combined,stop the moment no dry streaks remain. The batter is thin and the colour of wet slate.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin and bake on the middle shelf for 45 to 50 minutes, until the top is matt-set and a skewer pushed into the centre comes out with a few moist crumbs but no wet batter. The cake will look domed; it sinks slightly as it cools, which is correct.
Cool the cake in the tin for 30 minutes, then release the spring and transfer to a wire rack to cool completely. Frost only when fully cold,warm cake melts cream-cheese frosting.

Beat the cold cream cheese in a bowl with a balloon whisk until smooth,30 seconds. Sift in the icing sugar and beat again briefly. Pour in the cold double cream and whisk steadily until the frosting holds soft peaks and stands proud. Do not overwhip,cream cheese splits if it warms too far. Spread thickly over the top of the cooled cake, leaving the sides bare. Drag the spatula in soft swirls; the white-on-dark contrast does the work.
